perturbation.original_text: Garman agrees to indemnify Sparkling and hold it harmless from any and all losses, damages and expenses including without limitation, court costs, arbitration fees, penalties, fines, amounts paid in settlement of claims and reasonable legal fees and expenses of investigation (collectively the "Losses") which Sparkling or any of its respective officers or directors may incur due to a breach of this warranty.
perturbation.changed_text: Garman agrees to consider indemnifying Sparkling and endeavor to hold it harmless from losses, damages, and expenses. This consideration will encompass court costs, arbitration fees, penalties, fines, settlement amounts, and legal fees (collectively the 'Losses') which Sparkling or its officers/directors may face due to a warranty breach, subject to Garman's internal review process.
perturbation.explanation: The modification replaces 'agrees to indemnify' with 'agrees to consider indemnifying,' making the indemnification discretionary. The inclusion of 'subject to Garman's internal review process' introduces further ambiguity and gives Garman the power to deny indemnification even if a breach occurred. This weakens the original obligation.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Breach of Contract Remedies and Enforcement
perturbation.law_citation: Nova Scotia's Contract Law and the concept of 'Certainty of Terms'
perturbation.law_explanation: Nova Scotia's contract law requires certainty of terms, particularly regarding remedies for breach. The modified text introduces uncertainty about whether indemnification will actually occur, which could render the clause unenforceable. This contradicts the principle that contractual obligations should be clear and not subject to arbitrary internal review processes.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Nova Scotia Legislature - Legislation Skip to Navigation Skip to Content You are here Home » Legislative Business » Legislation Back to top Legislation The Legislature’s primary function is to make laws. Legislation is the entire body of documents related to the lawmaking process, which includes bills, acts or statutes, orders, and regulations. This site provides both current and historical legislation. The following documents and tools are available: Bills The status of bills table shows the progress of all bills introduced in the House of Assembly from 1995 to the present. Click on the bill title to view the first reading bill, Hansard debates, committee submissions, bill as passed, or the commencement dates for that bill. This page lists all proclamations, from February 22, 1990 to the present, alphabetically by statute title. View Proclamations Annual Statutes The Statutes of Nova Scotia from 1836 to the present and the Statutes at Large from 1758 to 1835 are all available in PDF. View Annual Statutes Revised Statutes (1851-2023) All versions of the revised statutes from 1851 to 2023 in PDF. Each revision brings together, or consolidates, the statutes to a given point in time. In the last revision, the Consolidation and Revision Officer was authorized to: alter the numbering and arrangement of the statutes and sections thereof; combine two or more statutes or parts thereof or subdivide any statute into two or more statutes; add, change or omit any title of a statute; change the language to preserve a uniform mode of expression; make minor amendments to bring out more clearly what is considered to be the intention of the Legislature or to reconcile seemingly inconsistent enactments or to correct clerical, typographical or printing errors; omit any forms or schedules contained in any statute and add authority to the statute for the forms of schedules to be prescribed by regulation. perturbation.justification: The modified text replaces 'agrees to indemnify' with 'agrees to consider indemnifying,' making the indemnification discretionary and subject to Garman's internal review process. The Law Explanation states that Nova Scotia's contract law requires certainty of terms, particularly regarding remedies for breach. The original text created a clear obligation, but the changed text introduces ambiguity, weakening the legal obligation and making it difficult to enforce, thereby contradicting the requirement for certainty. Scraped Law Snippet 1 describes legislation and statutes in Nova Scotia, which generally aim to create enforceable laws. The modification undermines this principle by making a key obligation uncertain.

perturbation.original_text: Garman shall provide to Sparkling as soon as reasonably available: (1) corrections and bug fixes for the Software; (2) all modifications, refinements, and enhancements ("Improvements") of the Software; (3) new releases of the Software; and (4) updated user manuals to support all of the above in a reasonable time frame.
perturbation.changed_text: Garman will aim to provide Sparkling corrections and bug fixes for the Software; modifications, refinements, and enhancements ('Improvements') of the Software; new releases of the Software; and updated user manuals to support all of the above, if deemed appropriate by Garman.
perturbation.explanation: The change weakens Garman's obligation to provide updates and support. Replacing 'shall provide' with 'will aim to provide' makes the obligation less mandatory. The added phrase 'if deemed appropriate by Garman' introduces significant ambiguity, allowing Garman to withhold updates at its discretion, contradicting the original intent of the maintenance agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Breach of Contract - Good Faith and Fair Dealing
perturbation.law_citation: Implied Duty of Good Faith in Contract Law (common law principle in Nova Scotia)
perturbation.law_explanation: Even without explicit statutory codification, Nova Scotia recognizes an implied duty of good faith and fair dealing in contractual performance. The modified text allows Garman to arbitrarily withhold updates and support, potentially undermining the value of the software license and violating this implied duty. By retaining complete discretion over providing updates, Garman could act in bad faith.
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity, allowing Garman to potentially withhold updates at its discretion. This contradicts the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing in contractual performance as explained in the Law Explanation. By changing 'shall provide' to 'will aim to provide' and adding 'if deemed appropriate by Garman', the obligation becomes significantly weaker, giving Garman the power to undermine the value of the software license. The Perturbation Explanation states that this change weakens Garman's obligation to provide updates, aligning with the definition of 'Ambiguities' creating a 'Legal Contradiction' when an obligation is introduced ambiguously making it difficult to enforce under state or national law. While the scraped snippets are unhelpful, the Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation sufficiently demonstrate the contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: In the event that the Telephone Support Service does provide a resolution to Sparkling (acting reasonably) within 2 business days to permit the Software to meet the Specifications, then Garman shall provide Software Repair Services to address the Software failure.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event that the Telephone Support Service provides what Garman considers a potential resolution to Sparkling within a timeframe Garman deems appropriate, Garman may, at its discretion, provide Software Repair Services.
perturbation.explanation: The original text sets a 2-day timeframe for resolution via telephone support, triggering a mandatory repair service. The modified version removes the concrete timeframe and substitutes 'what Garman considers a potential resolution' and 'a timeframe Garman deems appropriate,' making the obligation subjective. Replacing 'shall provide' with 'may, at its discretion, provide' makes the provision of repair services optional, regardless of the effectiveness of the telephone support or the urgency of the software failure. This contradicts the purpose of a maintenance agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Breach of Contract - Failure of Consideration
perturbation.law_citation: Failure of Consideration in Contract Law (common law principle in Nova Scotia)
perturbation.law_explanation: In Nova Scotia, as elsewhere, a contract requires consideration from both parties. Sparkling pays maintenance fees for a guaranteed level of support. The modified text allows Garman to avoid providing repair services even when telephone support is ineffective, potentially constituting a failure of consideration. By retaining complete discretion over providing repair services, Garman could be seen as not fulfilling its obligations under the contract, leading to a breach based on lack of consideration. This makes the contract significantly less valuable to Sparkling.
perturbation.justification: The original contract specified a 2-day timeframe for telephone support resolution, triggering a mandatory repair service. The changed text replaces this with 'what Garman considers a potential resolution' within 'a timeframe Garman deems appropriate,' and changes 'shall provide' to 'may, at its discretion, provide,' making repair services optional. The Law Explanation asserts this could be a failure of consideration, as Sparkling pays for a guaranteed level of support that Garman can now avoid providing, thus making the contract significantly less valuable to Sparkling. Since the ambiguity introduced allows Garman to avoid providing repair services even if telephone support is ineffective, it does create a contradiction. There is no scraped legal text to use.
